title: Thesis Notes
subtitle: Surya Narreddi · ITP Thesis
notes: Hi everyone. My name is Surya, and over the last year I've started using AI tools in my creative process.
And I found myself focusing on how quickly I could make something, and how many things I could make.

When I started to place emphasis on speed I let AI:

· fill in the gaps for me
· crystallise every creative kernel into a fully formed solution
· remove all friction, even meaningful friction, which can be a generative constraint
· offload my cognitive capacity for speed

And in the process, prompting AI began to resemble pulling levers on a slot machine.

Draining my attention, gambling tokens away.
But technology and attention are not antithetical.
So for my thesis I wanted to see: Where is the surface area for us to apply effort and devote our attention in a dignified manner when we use AI?

Today I'll be focusing on image generation with AI.

Where we prompt.

That goes into a model.

And we receive an image.

The one place we get to participate is at the prompt.
If all we can do is prompt, then we should probably pay attention. Right?

This is an image I generated using the prompt "A watercolour painting of a rainy day on the beach."

· looks pretty good, watercolour for sure
· but I did not ask for these mountains
· it's pouring out and these people are out for a walk?
· there's a dog??

This is the problem. It filled in gaps based on what little I provided.
At a talk recently, someone said prompting AI with an idea feels like describing a dream.
And it takes that fuzzy dream, and crystallises it into something fully formed, filling in the gaps with statistically likely things.

I used to be an architect.

We produced a lot of images, and they often started with dialogue with another person. We used to draw and speak our ideas over some paper.

Sometimes we'd just speak, when we were searching for an idea, and when we found it, we'd just draw.

There was meaning in what was said and unsaid.

In the end we'd be left with rich context and traces of our intent.
So I wanted to make something that felt like that, where I could draw, speak, and untangle my intent from an idea.

On the left, our first image from a prompt.

On the right, an image that came out of dialogue, where the exercise did not produce the image but the context and prompt for creating the final image.

You might be thinking you can do this by prompting back and forth.

Yes, but tools hold the first output tightly in context and you end up chipping at the edges, fighting tooth and nail to move it diagonally, or laterally.

No space for imperfect outputs. No space to untangle a fuzzy idea.

What I wanted was a tool that asked questions. A dreamy artefact that I could react to. Articulating what I wanted, and what I didn't. A session that produced rich context and traces.

A tool that lets me express intent without assuming intent for me.

That demo allowed me to devote my attention meaningfully in one part of the process.

But that's just one third of the whole. What about the rest?
How can we apply effort along the entire process?

I want to talk about Geoffrey Bawa's drawings briefly before we proceed.

· modernist architect from Sri Lanka, from the 1980s
· his studio drew trees on their architectural plans freehand, with ink pens, which was uncommon at the time

Look at the difference.

On the left, drafting pens, using rulers.

On the right, ink pens in freehand.

Tools have meaningful friction, which gives them opinions.
Ink pens don't give you the clinical line weights that drafting pens do, but they allow you to draw freely and really experience the tree while drawing.

And if you spend three hours drawing a tree, you can't treat it as an afterthought when constructing the actual building.

From the opinions and frictions of an ink pen, something really interesting happened.
Tropical Modernism, the architectural movement that dominates South East Asia to this day, was birthed because an architect decided to insert friction using an opinionated tool to make an image.
And so I wanted to see how could I introduce friction? How could I give AI tools, and maybe by doing so, something new could come from it?

I learned to code in p5.js, a JavaScript framework where you'd write code and it would produce an image.

p5 has a ton of libraries; my favourite is the brush library.

It's made by Alejandro, who also happens to be an architect.

It emulates the texture of drawing tools in code.

OK so. AI and language models I knew were amazing at code; it's their best medium.

And what if these libraries, like the brush library, could act as opinionated tools?

Maybe something new could come from it?

So I tried it.

The left is a language model writing code: words in, code out, and the code renders the image.

The right is an image model: prompt in, image out.

With image models you get a photograph of a dish and the recipe is withheld from you.

Generating images with code gives you the dish AND the recipe, and with the recipe you can cook too.

Since the output is code, you can tweak it, inspect it, and modify it to change the image.

We can participate at the artefact level with the final image, which you can't do with image models.

And you can also teach a model to get better at using the tools.

So I trained a small open model using Reinforcement Learning.

By giving it the libraries as tools and having it get better at using them.

The model writes p5.brush code, the code renders, and the output is evaluated.

Repeated over thousands of times, it learns and becomes better at painting with code.

For a large part of this process, the evaluation was me. Looking at thousands of images a night, scoring them, sending the signal back to the model.

I was a human-in-the-loop, tending to the model.

It got better because someone was paying attention and taking the time to help it learn.

The best part is, you can tune it to your preferences.

You can run it locally.

You can apply effort at the model level. Which was previously a black box and inaccessible.
These are some of my favourites.

This isn't a better way to make images. It's in fact slower. Much, much slower.

But now attention and effort lives across the prompt, the model, and the artefact.

There is surface area here for me to participate without surrendering the work entirely to AI.
I think this was possible because effort requires friction.
Gambling with a prompt is addictive and asks nothing of you.
Devotion requires our attention & effort.
So to answer my own question. Where is the surface area for a dignified attention and effort when working with AI?

The surface area is in the process of making the artefact.

The prompt. The model. The tools we choose. The hours we spend untangling fuzzy ideas. The places we decide to insert friction.

AI tools ask nothing of us. They are built to deliver polished average outputs.

To make the work truly yours, you have to devote your attention and effort to the process.
Thank you for your attention.
